text: Hello, I'm sharing another case of complicated diverticulitis.
This is a patient who had an episode of a perforation several months ago.
And although most of their symptoms had resolved, persistent imaging had shown ongoing inflammation and even a small abscess between sigmoid and the uterus, which you'll see later in the case.
And she was counseled on the risks and benefits and chose to undergo elective resection.
The surgeon uses cold scissor dissection to carefully separate the small bowel adhered to the right adnexa and the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon.
I'm taking a look at it. It looked okay.
The surgeon begins the planned medial to lateral dissection, focusing on separating the sigmoid colon from adjacent structures.
I always like to see the ureter before I will take the vessel. It's just nice practice.
I feel that having the ICG in the stents saves a lot of time.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) proximal to the takeoff of the left colic artery, with both structures visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a small tunnel and uses a vessel sealer to relieve tension and divide the vessel.
The surgeon continues the dissection from the medial space, progressing until the view becomes obstructed.
The surgeon places a sponge in the surgical field and proceeds to perform lateral dissection.
The surgeon enters the medially dissected plane, with a visible sponge placed in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to lift the mesentery away from the kidney, revealing the anatomical separation between these structures.
The surgeon approaches the splenic flexure, switching to the vessel sealer to enter the plane between the splenic flexure and the left kidney.

The patient remains in a steep Trendelenburg position with a slight left side elevation. The surgeon utilizes the current port configuration to achieve a clear view, successfully mobilizing the entire splenic flexure.
The inferior border of the pancreas becomes visible as the surgeon continues the dissection near the splenic flexure.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure using an inferior lateral approach, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues to mobilize tissue along the base of the pancreas, maintaining the dissection plane established earlier.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to separate diseased tissue from the left pelvic sidewall, then proceeds to dissect the tissue off the dome of the bladder and uterus.
A small cavity is visible, currently filled with granulation tissue.
The surgeon has freed the colon, preparing for transrectal extraction of the specimen.
The surgeon clears the rectal margin of its mesentery, followed by clearing the proximal margin, ensuring both areas are free from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon advances the dissection up to the valve wall and checks perfusion by marking with cautery both proximally and distally to ensure a proper transection site.
The surgeon divides the upper rectum using scissors, carefully cutting through the tissue to minimize thermal spread.

The surgeon begins to chop the colon specimen into smaller pieces, removing the mesentery first. Without using an Alexis wound protector, the surgeon manually extracts each piece individually, moving between the surgical site and the patient's legs to retrieve the specimens.
The surgeon attempts to cut through dense, rock-like specimens using a vessel sealer, but switches to scissors for more effective cutting.
The surgeon identifies the main part of the diseased tissue and proceeds to transect it into two final pieces. Each piece is carefully removed to prevent any from flipping into the upper abdomen.
The surgeon begins a sewn two-layer anastomosis, focusing on the back outer row of sutures to connect the bowel segments.

The surgeon performs a sewn two-layer anastomosis, joining seromuscular bites a few millimeters from the edge of the transected bowel wall. The outer back row is completed, and the inner back row is started using 3-0 nine-inch V-Lux sutures.

The surgeon continues the inner back row suture, starting in the middle and sewing towards the near corner. The stitch is turned around the far corner, transitioning to the inner anterior row. A canal suture is used to imbricate the lip, but due to challenging angles, the far suture is terminated and completed with the nearsight suture.
The surgeon ties two suture lines together and then takes the original posterior outer row, sewing it around to the anterior side.

The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis, ensuring the integrity of the bowel connection. The surgical field shows four eight millimeter ports, with the right lower quadrant port closed using a suture passer to prevent hernias.

The surgeon uses a 19mm rigid practice scope to inspect the anastomosis, confirming the absence of bubbles and bleeding, and ensuring the anastomosis is widely patent. The surgical field is then cleaned up, marking the completion of the procedure.
